articles.body: Spain reunited with their supporters in Madrid less than 24 hours after winning the World Cup final against Argentina (1-0, after extra time). The players of La Roja, alongside head coach Luis de la Fuente, presented the trophy upon their arrival at the airport before taking part in celebrations across the capital. More than 1 million people attended the parade, which ended with a major concert at Cibeles Square.
articles.headline: World Cup 2026 : Spain return to Madrid in triumph

articles.body: Britain's new Prime Minister Andy Burnham took office on Monday, reshuffling the cabinet to remove allies of predecessor Keir Starmer and appointing John Healey as finance minister, Ed Miliband as foreign minister and Wes Streeting as defence minister. Burnham pledged a 10-year plan to tackle cost-of-living pressures and signalled a leftward shift.
articles.headline: UK Prime Minister Andy Burnham unveils new government, replacing Starmer era

articles.body: France's parliament is set to approve a social media ban for children under 15 on Tuesday after lawmakers from the upper and lower houses reached a compromise on the bill's text Monday. If the bill is approved, France will become the first country in Europe to put such a ban in place.
articles.headline: French lawmakers expected to pass social media ban for children under 15

articles.body: Lebanese President Joseph Aoun is expected to present a plan to his US counterpart Donald Trump Tuesday on how to disarm Iran-backed militant group Hezbollah, which Israel has made a key condition of its withdrawal from the lands that its troops occupy in the country's south.
articles.headline: Lebanon's Aoun to press Trump for Israeli troop withdrawal as pressure builds to disarm Hezbollah

articles.headline: World Cup 2026: for New York souvenir shops, the impact is mixed

articles.body: At least 27 people died after a ferry capsized off Guyana, Prime Minister Mark Phillips said Monday, as rescuers searched for 83 people still missing. Sixty-nine passengers have been rescued since the MV Barima overturned Saturday night while traveling from Georgetown to the inland Essequibo region, Phillips said.
articles.headline: Guyana ferry capsizes, leaving dozens dead and scores missing

articles.headline: 2026 World Cup: La Roja celebrates title with victory parade in Madrid

articles.body: US President Donald Trump signed orders on Monday imposing new 50% tariffs on some Canadian goods, citing what the White House called Canada's "discriminatory treatment" of US alcohol, automobiles and dairy products. The tariffs take effect in 30 days, with energy, potash and sector-specific imports exempted, the White House said.
articles.headline: Trump signs order imposing new 50% tariffs on certain Canadian goods

articles.body: The Lebanese army began assuming security responsibilities in three southern villages on Monday under a US-backed pilot initiative, the State Department said. Operations in Froun, Srifa and Zawtar al-Gharbiya mark the first implementation of a framework aimed at enabling a phased Israeli withdrawal from southern Lebanon and Hezbollah's disarmament.
articles.headline: Lebanese army deploys to three southern villages under US-backed security framework

articles.body: In tonight's edition, the Senegalese President Bassirou Diomaye Faye takes the helm of ECOWAS. Also, in Nigeria nearly 19 million Nigerian children are out of school, with the threat of kidnapping keeping many classrooms empty. And Tangier is celebrating the art of visual storytelling with the International Photography Festival.
articles.headline: Senegalese president assumes ECOWAS leadership

articles.body: Spain’s Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez on Monday visited Algeria for the first time in four years in a bid to mend diplomatic ties between the two countries after they were frayed by a row over Western Sahara. In 2022, Madrid abandoned its neutrality stance over the disputed territory and backed Morocco’s sovereignty claim, angering Algiers. The thaw began last year, when Algeria reactivated a friendship treaty it had suspended over the dispute.
articles.headline: Spain PM Sanchez visits Algeria for first time in four years as ties thaw

articles.body: The Senegalese government on Monday made a U-turn, announcing it would back former president Macky Sall’s candidacy to replace UN Secretary-General Antonio Guterres when his term expires. The government had previously declined to throw its weight behind Sall, whom it accuses of deadly political repression while in office, but reversed course after he paid the sitting president a visit in Dakar this weekend.
articles.headline: Senegal changes course to back ex-leader Macky Sall as UN chief

articles.body: Speaking with FRANCE 24's Sharon Gaffney, Martin Farr, Senior Lecturer in Contemporary British History at Newcastle University, explains that "the Labour Party members and its MPs think that it's their responsibility to prevent" the rise to power of right-wing populist party Reform UK, and that has motivated the decision to replace Keir Starmer with Andy Burnham at 10 Downing Street.
articles.headline: 'Labour thinks it has responsibility to prevent the rise of Reform UK', historian says

articles.body: François Picard is pleased to welcome Mary Honeyball, former Labour Member of the European Parliament from 2000 to 2019 representing London. She offers a cautiously optimistic assessment of the arrival of Andy Burnham to 10 Downing Street. She frames the moment less as a potential political reset after years of institutional fatigue. Mary Honeyball argues that the significance of the new premiership lies in the possibility of restoring public confidence, rebalancing the relationship between central and local government, and offering a more hopeful national narrative after the political and economic dislocation associated with Brexit.
articles.headline: Mary Honeyball praises forward-looking PM 'prepared to inject some optimism' into United Kingdom

articles.body: France’s Foreign Minister Jean-Noël Barrot on Monday said two French embassy officials had been subject to an “extremely serious act of intimidation” by Iranian security services in Iran the day before, having been held and interrogated for hours. One of them was also subjected to physical abuse, he said.
articles.headline: Two French diplomats subjected to ‘extremely serious act of intimidation’ in Iran, FM Barrot says

articles.body: Andy Burnham has become Britain's new prime minister. The nomination process unfolded on July 20th in London, with one outgoing Labour leader passing the baton to another. The hope is that Burnham will ease the leadership turnover, stabilising the country.
articles.headline: 'That is our challenge: to make politics work': Andy Burnham becomes UK PM

articles.body: The United States and Iran have continued to exchange fire on July 20th, raising fears for Gulf shipping and water supplies in the region. After Kuwait reported Iranian attacks on water and electricity plants as well as oil facilities over the weekend, Bahrain said Iran this Monday, July 20th attacked civil air navigation systems. Despite this, Tehran says communication with Washington remains active through diplomatic channels.
articles.headline: Fears over Gulf shipping and water rise as US-Iran trade strikes

articles.body: Eve Irvine is pleased to welcome Robbie Knock, Lead Commentator and sports journalist. The 2026 FIFA World Cup will be remembered not only for Spain's triumph on the pitch but also for the way football became enmeshed in politics, entertainment, institutional scrutiny, and the closing chapter of one of the sport's greatest careers. Robbie Knock argues that Spain's victory was the product of tactical superiority rather than fortune, while acknowledging that controversial refereeing decisions complicated perceptions of the tournament. He also presents Lionel Messi's expected international farewell as the conclusion of a defining era in world football, emphasising that dignity in defeat may ultimately reinforce rather than diminish his legacy.
articles.headline: Expanded 2026 North American World Cup: Spain's 'diligent' performance to Messi's enduring legacy

articles.body: Ukraine fired more than 400 drones toward Moscow in its latest major attack on the Russian capital on July 20. The blitz came hours after Russia bombarded Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities with ballistic missiles in a relentless cycle of tit-for-tat strikes.
articles.headline: Ukraine fires 400 missiles at Moscow after deadly Russian attacks

articles.body: Annette Young is pleased to welcome Tim Bale, a Professor of Politics at Queen Mary University in London. Andy Burnham inherits the Labour leadership at a moment when Britain's political debate is defined less by ideology than by structural constraints. Professor Bale argues that Burnham's success will depend on navigating three interconnected dilemmas: restoring economic growth to unlock fiscal capacity, rebuilding public confidence in the state's ability to manage migration, and gradually redefining Britain's post Brexit relationship with Europe without reopening politically divisive debates prematurely.
articles.headline: Burnham Advocates Closer EU Relationship: Big Gains Will Only Come from Rejoining the Single Market

articles.body: Firebrand Republican Rep. Nancy Mace announced Monday that she will not run for the South Carolina Senate seat held by the late Lindsey Graham. “I'm not running for the U.S. Senate, not because I'm backing down from a fight, but because the one I'm already in matters right now,” she said in a video announcement . Her decision comes after a fifth-place primary finish in her run for governor earlier this year and as the race to succeed Graham is already taking shape. Mace began exploring the viability of a potential bid just hours after Graham’s death became public, two people familiar with her thinking told POLITICO at the time, but decided against a second statewide bid in as many months. Her move effectively guarantees she will not be serving in elected office once her term ends early next year. In the video, Mace said “the laws meant to protect me and other women failed us, and I won't stop until that changes.” “Serving South Carolina has been the honor of my life, and I will continue to voice our values everywhere I can; I’m even going to shit post from time to time, because someone has to call out the dumbasses,” she said. Sen. Darline Graham , Lindsey Graham’s sister who was appointed to serve out the remainder of his term shortly after his death, announced Monday that she is running for a full term, and she has already secured President Donald Trump’s endorsement. GOP Reps. Russell Fry and Ralph Norman have announced bids as well. The filing window for the special primary election opens Tuesday and closes one week later. The election will be held Aug. 11, with a runoff for the top two candidates if no one wins 50 percent of the vote Aug. 25. Whoever wins the special GOP primary will be the heavy favorite to secure a full term in November.
articles.headline: Nancy Mace won’t run for Lindsey Graham’s Senate seat

articles.body: Saudi Arabia’s 2034 World Cup may not be a 2034 World Cup at all. The tournament is unlikely to be played in the summer — and the calendar math could push it into 2035. FIFA has, of course, switched the World Cup to winter before to avoid playing games in intense summer heat, with Qatar’s 2022 tournament the first to ever break from seasonal tradition. But that Gulf precedent won’t carry to Saudi neatly. In 2034, Ramadan is slated to fall around Nov. 12 through Dec. 12 — squarely in the late-year window that Qatar used. In Muslim countries, Ramadan does far more than shuffle mealtimes and deepen prayer. The rhythm of public life bends completely, with social, civic and work schedules pushed later into a day defined by the night. A Saudi World Cup during Ramadan would solve one problem only by creating another. Alternative slots in 2034 are not much more forgiving. January and February would run into Salt Lake City’s Winter Olympics, splitting audience attention, sponsor dollars and media oxygen. Then around early March comes Hajj, a massive logistical undertaking that brings roughly 2 million pilgrims from across the world to Saudi for a highly coordinated week of rituals. Unlike a typical event on the global calendar, Hajj cannot simply be moved — its timing is set by the Islamic lunar calendar. Anything after that starts to nudge the tournament out of the cooler season that made summer untenable in the first place. All of this primes an option that otherwise sounds absurd: early 2035. FIFA has not indicated that it is considering such a move, but the calendar constraints make it a scenario that cannot be easily dismissed. The cleanest stretch looks to be between the Christmas-New Year’s time frame, which FIFA is sure to avoid, and Hajj, expected that year in mid-February. That leaves a plausible, though still narrow, six to seven weeks to stage a tournament across Saudi’s 15 proposed stadiums, possibly with 64 teams. That would be a tighter squeeze than this year’s 48 teams at 16 stadiums over five and some weeks. The scheduling bind sharpens a test of hierarchy in the global calendar: which dates become constraints and which become variables. The likelihood of another winter World Cup is already reviving 2022 resentment, especially in Europe, where it will interrupt club seasons. And that prospect feeds into a larger unease over Saudi as host, from human rights concerns that hang over the kingdom to the unusual circumstances surrounding its selection . FIFA has spent years trying to present Qatar, and now Saudi Arabia, as proof that world football’s governing body is becoming less parochial and more global. That framing also serves a commercial purpose, courting football audiences and markets beyond FIFA’s traditional centers of power. A World Cup competing with the host country’s religious observances would put all of that under obvious strain. Even Muslims with no affection for the Saudi state, including those well beyond the Gulf, are likely to view any FIFA conflict with Ramadan or Hajj as an overstep. Because the Islamic calendar is lunar, Ramadan and Hajj drift roughly ten days earlier each year, constantly shifting their collision points with global sports seasons. Hajj is a Saudi-specific factor, but Ramadan reaches across the Muslim world, a growing presence among host countries: Qatar in 2022, Morocco as part of 2030, Saudi Arabia in 2034. If that sequence hints at FIFA’s future, these calendar conflicts foreshadow a recurring tension. This year’s North American tournament has also revived a broader question: whether the summer months can remain the World Cup’s permanent home as temperatures rise. That could eventually force countries outside the Gulf to begin reconsidering their own seasonal blueprint, making Saudi 2034 less an anomaly and more a preview of what’s to come. Last October, FIFA President Gianni Infantino said that the soccer world should be more open-minded about tournament timing — suggesting that a game spread across climates and continents cannot expect to remain tied to the traditional June-July template.
articles.headline: Will the 2034 cup be in 2035?

articles.body: Before the final whistle blows on the 2026 World Cup, a familiar tension is already bubbling ahead of its next two installments. Morocco, which will co-host the 2030 World Cup alongside Spain and Portugal, criminalizes same-sex sexual activity. And in Saudi Arabia, host of the 2034 tournament, homosexuality is understood to be illegal under Islamic law. Both countries submitted plans to address the potential human rights risks associated with hosting the tournament as part of their bids, but human rights advocates say the nations must guarantee that queer fans are not prosecuted under those laws. And they say FIFA, which has an ostensible commitment to fighting discrimination in football, should play a role in securing that. “It doesn’t mean that FIFA is the world police and somehow has the power to tell countries what to do with their laws,” said Steve Cockburn, Amnesty International’s head of labor rights and sports. “But it can certainly engage and use its leverage to put in place measures that would be protective of that.” The debate over what role FIFA should play in promoting human rights, and how far it should go to do so, is familiar to most fans of international soccer. FIFA drew criticism from international spectators when it announced ahead of the 2022 World Cup in Qatar that it would deliver a yellow card to team captains who donned the multi-colored “OneLove armband,” while the Qatari Emir said that all would be welcome so long as they respect the country’s culture. Critics also drew attention to Russian laws prohibiting so-called gay propaganda ahead of the 2018 contest. Morocco’s human rights strategy document notes that its independent human rights committee and the Moroccan Football Federation would “encourage both the parliament and the government to ratify key international conventions,” broadly referring to widely understood human rights standards, though the document did not explicitly mention discrimination on the basis of sexual orientation. Saudi Arabia’s proposal similarly makes no explicit mention of addressing discrimination on the basis of sexual orientation, but asserts that the federation would “continuously verify that our legislations and practices align with our commitments to various international agreements pertaining to anti-discrimination and implement any required enhancements.” The nation’s tourism ministry says on its website that it welcomes all people, but it asks visitors to “respect our culture and traditions and follow our laws as they would when visiting any other country in the world.” “Like other governments around the world, visitors are not required to disclose their personal information and we will respect visitors’ right to privacy,” its tourism FAQs site continues. The FIFA Congress, made up of its member federations, approved both uncontested bids. FIFA and the Saudi and Moroccan embassies in Washington, D.C., did not respond to requests for comment. Cockburn, whose organization authored a 2024 report slamming both the Moroccan and Saudi human rights strategies, said FIFA must go to greater lengths to guarantee that queer fans are protected while attending the games. That’s on top of the slew of concerns directed specifically at Saudi Arabia over workers’ rights and a lack of a minimum wage, contained in its report. Countries regularly guarantee bits of policy to FIFA during negotiations to host World Cups, including special tax arrangements and altered visa protocols. “There needs to be a bit more of an upfront, honest, grown up conversation about this, so that everyone’s clear, that it’s not fudged and that fans are not left guessing about what the situation is and how to look after themselves,” Cockburn said. The arrival of the world’s most watched sporting event in those two nations can contribute to “a fair degree of, sort of, cynicism or general, sort of, distrust” among some LGBT-rights advocacy groups that FIFA sometimes doesn’t take this issue on in a systematic way that we would all like to see, said executive director of FIFA’s anti-discrimination partner Fare network Piara Powar. “They just seem very kind of cautious around it and sadly it’s just not really taking us anywhere,” Jon Holmes, spokesperson for the group Football v Homophobia, said of FIFA. If history is an indication, however, it’s possible that such concerns over human rights at the World Cup could fade once the international drama of the tournament begins. Such was the case in the U.S., where concerns over potential ICE raids, overwhelmed logistical systems and more — which did cause some to boycott the games — largely faded away amid a wildly successful tournament. “We all kind of get lost in the event, and the narratives, and the storylines, and the athletes, and the beautiful game, and the cultures all coming together,” said Ryan Turcott, an associate professor at Gonzaga University who studies the politics of international sports. “But we lose track of the impact and the legacy when it comes to improving human rights.” CORRECTION: A previous version of this report misspelled Ryan Turcott's name. CORRECTION: An earlier version of this report misstated the year of the Amnesty International report on Moroccan and Saudi human rights strategies. The report was from 2024.
articles.headline: Moroccan and Saudi World Cups draw human rights concerns

articles.body: FIFA is expecting record-breaking revenue from the 2026 World Cup. But it’s still leaving a lot of money on the table — or more specifically, Chinese yuan. Despite the expanded field in this year’s tournament, China’s men’s national team still failed to qualify. FIFA is now talking about expanding again to 64 teams in 2030, with the implicit hope of pulling in the Chinese team and its massive market of soccer-obsessed fans. The running joke in Chinese online soccer forums is that the men’s national team may still not qualify, says Hong Kong University Professor Xu Gouqi, who wrote a 2008 book on the role of sports in China. “Chinese are not allowed to discuss many things in social media,” Xu noted in an interview. “But you can criticize the Chinese men’s football team with no consequences. It’s still a safe place.” In fairness, the men’s team has not given its fans much reason for optimism over, well, the last century. The squad was an Asian powerhouse back in the 1930s, but has only qualified for the World Cup once, in 2002, when it failed to score a goal in losing all three of its games. It’s not for lack of trying. Xi Jinping rode to power in China fresh off helming the wildly successful 2008 Beijing Olympics, and he’s keenly aware of how sports are intertwined with China’s sense of identity on the world stage, says Xu. One of Xi’s early promises: rebuilding the men’s national team to put it on a path to winning the World Cup by 2050, which is aligned with the Chinese Communist Party’s long-term blueprint for turning the country into a fully-modernized global superpower. So far, it hasn’t been going great. In the Asian qualifying tournament for the 2026 World Cup, the Chinese men’s team got pummeled 7-0 in one game against regional power Japan, while losing to Indonesia and tying Singapore (neither of which qualified, either). There are currently no Chinese men’s players playing in the top tiers of any of Europe’s professional leagues, a key metric of elite talent. Experts detail a number of reasons for the failures, including corruption and the CCP’s top-down approach to athletics. At a fundamental level, it comes down to the lack of grassroots participation, says Rowan Simons, a U.K.-born, Beijing-based author of a book on soccer in China and founder of ClubFootball Beijing. “China still needs to concentrate on building the football culture that is shared by all nations playing in the World Cup finals. This is built on large numbers actually participating in the game,” Simons said in an e-mail. As it stands, registered youth soccer participation in China is still just a fraction of countries with far smaller populations, like England. Xu agreed that while there is “a world class football fan base” in China, “you don’t get kids kicking the ball around” for fun the way you would see in say, South America or Europe. Parents view it as a waste of time “because the chance isn’t that great to succeed,” he said. Going to the best university is viewed as a more assured path. But unlike Simons, Xu is optimistic that the Chinese men’s team can not just qualify but win the World Cup in the coming decades, which he says would bring out a whole new level of soccer obsession in the country of 1.4 billion — a FIFA marketer’s dream. The answer, he says, lies in an approach China recently used to achieve Olympic success. Call it the Eileen Gu model. Gu, the daughter of a first-generation Chinese immigrant mother, was born and raised in Northern California but competes for China in freestyle skiing. Her gold and silver medals at the Winter Olympics in Milan-Cortina earlier this year made her one of the most decorated freestyle skiers in Olympic history. “We just simply bring the best and brightest from the kid level” to China from other countries, said Xu. That would represent “not just a major breakthrough in sports,” he argued, but “a major breakthrough in the idea of China.”
articles.headline: The World Cup's missing country

articles.body: The World Cup final wrapped up today in the portmandu place FIFA calls New York New Jersey. The two states, which shared responsibilities to host eight matches and the final, did so through an uneasy alliance . There were tit-for-tats over which state should get first billing on signs outside the stadium . Their two governors – both Democrats – released dueling transportation plans. Now, there is at least some evidence that if there’s a chance for them to work together again, New York may be reluctant to pair up with New Jersey – and if it does, officials may want to iron things out ahead of time. The two border states are thrown together for major sporting events because New York City is a global tourist destination with plenty of hotels, nightlife and attractions. But it has no major sporting venue that can hold more than 50,000 fans. Neighboring New Jersey has MetLife Stadium, which seats 80,000. The most public spat between the co-hosts came when New Jersey Gov. Mikie Sherrill said she planned to charge $150 to carry fans to MetLife Stadium for matches, a price she later lowered to $98. She portrayed the high ticket price as an affordability-minded decision to avoid making her residents subsidize World Cup-goers who may have thousands of dollars sitting around to pay for their seats. She also castigated FIFA for refusing to chip in to help pay for transportation. But New York Gov. Kathy Hochul went the opposite direction and helped lower the cost of a special bus to the matches to $20, viewing affordability in a different framework: how much it cost to get to the match. At the time, one New York lawmaker, Assemblymember Robert Carroll, who represents Brooklyn, said on X that decisions like Sherrill’s “ensure that the next time New York City hosts the world, partnering with New Jersey won’t even be on the table.” In June, Hochul tapped Carroll to help evaluate whether New York should bid for the 2042 Winter Olympics. In an interview before the World Cup final, Carroll said he didn’t anticipate working with New Jersey for the Olympics, because events could take place in the city and in Lake Placid, which has already hosted two Winter Olympics. Still, Carroll offered a more nuanced view than his social media post. He said the two states are linked for some events. But he remained critical of Sherrill and said things like the New Jersey Transit fares she imposed should be discussed if there is planning for a women’s World Cup, Super Bowl or other major event that involves both states. “We should not try to harm one another,” Carroll said. “I think that it was very foolish for the governor to kind of put up a punitive tax towards New Yorkers or New York tourists going to World Cup games. And that was a poor decision and I think it shouldn't happen in the future.” Sherrill’s office referred a request for comment about Carroll’s comments to her previous remarks, but in a separate interview with POLITICO, the governor accused FIFA of trying not to pay its bills . There is also at least some thinking in New York that the city will have a place to hold women’s World Cup matches in 2031, when the U.S. is expected to host the tournament. A 25,000-seat professional soccer stadium, Etihad Park, is set to open next year in Queens. New York City’s top economic development official, Jeanny Pak, said during a recent event at the French consulate that she’d like to look at hosting the women’s tournament at the new stadium. “We’re really looking forward to that,” Pak said. “I think we're really well poised to be the right place for it, so spread the word.”
articles.headline: Will New York, New Jersey do sports together ever again?

articles.body: There was another World Cup victory on Sunday — for one of the nation's largest transit agencies. New Jersey Transit, which became infamous for disarray outside MetLife Stadium following the 2014 Super Bowl, carried soccer fans on its buses and trains there and back eight times since the World Cup began — without major incident. For anyone in the New York City metropolitan area used to experiencing or hearing gripes about New Jersey's transit system — which a former governor repeatedly promised to fix , even if it killed him — this was as surprising as almost anything that happened on the soccer pitch. "The curse of Super Bowl 2014 is over," New Jersey Transit CEO Kris Kolluri said Sunday evening during an interview inside the agency's command center in the MetLife parking lot. That Super Bowl featured extreme delays getting American football fans in and out of the New Jersey swamp where the stadium is located. During this soccer tournament, the agency did end up carrying fewer passengers than expected, and the reasons for that remain up for debate. The agency has blamed the unexpected availability of parking spaces for fans at a nearby mall, as well as a cheaper bus service subsidized by New York Gov. Kathy Hochul. Others blame New Jersey itself because Gov. Mikie Sherrill decided the state would charge soccer fans $98 to get to games. Whatever the case, the system ran without any major meltdowns during matches despite some extreme weather that threatened to upend plans and affected other service. Kolluri, who spoke with reporters while surrounded by some of his team that's been working on the World Cup for several years, turned the interview into a pep talk for the squad. As he spoke, trains were coming and going from MetLife handling the remnants of World Cup fans and, back in New York City — where most people were heading — there was a Yankees game and Bon Jovi concert at Madison Square Garden. Still, a screen over his shoulder with video feeds from bus and train platforms across the region showed things working smoothly. "The only reason why this worked is because of an extraordinary team who knew we were the underdog, and we were not going to let each other down," Kolluri said. The agency is still figuring out how much it cost to provide the special World Cup service, which involved extraordinary measures, including having 80 train cars available to whisk trains away from the stadium.
articles.headline: NJ Transit achieves its World Cup goooal!

articles.body: CHICAGO — The United States’ third-largest city felt confident passing on this year’s World Cup in part because the city’s sports loyalty was well-scripted. The Bears owned Sundays. The Cubs and White Sox divided baseball fans. And the Bulls still carry the glow of the Jordan dynasty. But a whole new generation of sports teams is betting Chicago’s appetite for sports is bigger than its traditional loyalties — and that fans are ready for something different. The WNBA’s Chicago Sky is preparing to host the league’s All-Star weekend July 23-26. The Chicago Stars are trying to build a women’s soccer franchise in a city with deep youth participation and a growing appetite for the sport. And the Chicago Fire are planning a new privately financed stadium in the South Loop and are investing heavily in youth development and facilities. The timing is notable. The city’s newest teams are making their pitch at the same moment two of Chicago’s oldest franchises are asking for help building new homes. The McCaskey family continues to pursue public assistance for a new Bears stadium, whether in Illinois or Indiana. White Sox owner Jerry Reinsdorf has sought public support for a proposed South Loop ballpark. Those requests have triggered a familiar debate: whether taxpayers should help finance stadium projects for billion-dollar sports franchises while governments struggle with schools, transit and other basic services. The newer teams are offering a different message. Chicago Fire owner Joe Mansueto, another Chicago billionaire, privately funded the team’s $750 million stadium, though the Chicago City Council earlier this week approved a $425 million tax subsidy to fund the surrounding infrastructure. Their pitch is not built around nostalgia but around investing in communities. At a sports and business forum at the Obama Presidential Center this week, Fire Chief Marketing Officer Dan Moriarty described a shift in how soccer is being consumed in Chicago. The sport’s audience is younger, more diverse and increasingly global, he said, with fans who already follow international clubs now looking for a local connection. He pointed to a simple sign of change: at Chicago farmers markets, soccer jerseys are increasingly common — even if many of them still carry the names of European clubs. The challenge, he said, is turning those fans into supporters of Chicago teams. “Soccer has this lifestyle aspect,” former U.S. women’s national team goalkeeper Alyssa Naeher said during the discussion. “It brings people together.” That may be soccer’s biggest advantage in Chicago. The city is already a global crossroads, with neighborhoods shaped by generations of immigrants and communities that have long embraced the sport. The Fire are betting that global stars can accelerate that connection. The club recently signed Polish striker Robert Lewandowski, one of the world’s most accomplished players, as part of a broader effort to raise the team’s profile. Women’s sports are riding a similar wave. “Women’s sports generally, and particularly soccer, has seen an evolution in this country,” said Laura Ricketts, who is a bridge to the old and new in sports. She’s a co-owner of the Chicago Cubs, an investor in the WNBA’s Chicago Sky and executive director of the Chicago Stars women’s soccer team. “What we’re now seeing [is] an explosion, especially in the NWSL,” she said of the National Women’s Soccer League. “We’re growing, and the best way [to see it is] our fan base is growing.” She compares women’s soccer today to the NBA or Major League Baseball of the early 1980s. “There’s just so much opportunity,” said Ricketts. Women’s soccer, she said, has benefited from decades of investment in youth leagues and college programs. That foundation is now translating into a professional audience. The growth is not just about attendance. It is about loyalty. Women’s sports fans, Ricketts said, tend to be younger and highly engaged, with a strong connection to the brands and communities supporting the teams. With World Cup interest, “We’re just seeing the tip of the iceberg for soccer in this country.” She said there’s been similar investment into basketball and she hopes Chicago sees the benefits of that as it hosts the WNBA All-Star weekend, marking three decades of the league. “The momentum continues right here in Chicago,” Sky co-owner Nadia Rawlinson said, describing the event as a celebration of how far women’s basketball has come — and how much further it can go. None of this means the Bears, Cubs, White Sox or Bulls are losing their place in Chicago’s sports hierarchy. But newer teams are making a case for a place alongside them.
articles.headline: Chicago passed on the World Cup but has other sports plans

articles.body: New York and New Jersey planned to spend roughly a half billion dollars hosting eight World Cup matches, including today’s final. But once it’s over, lawmakers in both states want to know if they got their money’s worth. In New York City, Councilmember Virginia Maloney, who leads the chamber’s committee on economic development, is going to lead that examination. “First I want to make sure we recouped our cost,” she said in an interview on Friday at a coffee shop in her Manhattan district, which includes the Upper East Side. “I'm not confident we can say that.” She said data seems mixed on how well hotels did because of the World Cup. Bookings initially appeared low, but it seems like hotels recovered closer to the matches. However, it’s not clear if that’s because of the World Cup or the other major events happening this summer in the city, including the July 4th festivities and the Knicks’ championship win — which means an analysis will have to try to untangle what economic activity happened because of the World Cup and what would have happened anyway. “What is irrefutable is that bars and restaurants were seeing a lot more activity and action in New York, and that translates into real economic benefit for New Yorkers,” Maloney said. Maloney attributes that to a lot of things, including a law signed by Gov. Kathy Hochul to temporarily extend hours for bars and restaurants. One report from the Queens Chamber of Commerce found a 30 percent increase in businesses for people going out to drink and eat. “The economic benefit was not necessarily people who were going to the World Cup, but just people participating in the summer of New York, going to all five boroughs to celebrate or be out together,” she said. The host committee released a study last summer predicting $3.3 billion in economic impact across New York and New Jersey, including about $432 million in state and local tax revenues. Maloney worried about rosy projections from FIFA and its boosters compared to the reality on the ground. A preliminary study by the host committee of just the group stage found $1.2 billion in direct economic impact, Bloomberg reported . Maloney views the experience as a learning opportunity for other big events the city puts on, including future political conventions and the 2042 Olympics, which the state and city are exploring a bid for. “It should be something that you can make a playbook out of, not where you're starting from scratch every time there's a major event,” Maloney said.
articles.headline: New York, New Jersey to examine whether World Cup funds were money well spent

articles.body: White House FIFA Taskforce executive director Andrew Giuliani told me on Friday that he wanted this match to go to overtime. You might have called the overtime, I told him as the players prepared to return to the field after 90 minutes. “We can thank Martinez for playing out of his mind!!” he texted back, in reference to Argentina's goalkeeper Emiliano Martinez.
articles.headline: It's Giuliani (extra) time

articles.body: President Donald Trump is watching the World Cup from a box with First Lady Melania Trump, Eric Trump and Barron Trump, the true soccer fan in the Trump family. Trump is with fellow North American leaders Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum and Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney. The trio is meeting in-person for just the second time (their first meeting was at the FIFA final draw in Washington). Also joining Trump in his box are King Felipe VI and Queen Letizia of Spain and Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez, who has clashed with Trump on defense spending and Spain’s refusal to allow the U.S. to use its military bases in its strikes against Iran. FIFA President Gianni Infantino is there of course, along with White House FIFA Taskforce executive director Andrew Giuliani, Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick, Secretary of State Marco Rubio, DHS Secretary Markwayne Mullin, Patriots owner Bob Kraft and Brian Kilmeade from Fox News.
articles.headline: All the president's guests

articles.body: Shakira, Madonna, Justin Bieber, BTS and a coitier of other musicians and dancers just wrapped up a very Gianni Infantino coded half-time show at MetLife Stadium. Across the performances, but especially in visual displays on the field, there were snippets of his vision of soccer as a global unifying force. Dancers carrying colorful globes and flags with hearts on them. A series of letters came together to spell out "LOVE" with an image of the Earth as the "O." Much of the halftime show took place atop a tarp covering the whole field with words and phrases in multiple languages, like "Purpose," "Grow" "Future," "Inspire," and "We are all one in the universe." Many of the fans stayed in their seats to watch.
articles.headline: A very Infantino-coded show

articles.body: Forget Zohran Mamdani. The local elected official who really rules MetLife Stadium today is Jeffrey Lahullier, whose 10,000-person town sees its population swell by more than 800 percent whenever there’s an event at the stadium complex in the middle of his turf, writes POLITICO's Will McCarthy: Read Will's story “The World Cup came to town. The town grew eight times larger” here .
articles.headline: The World Cup final came to town. The town grew 8 times larger.

articles.body: With the World Cup putting soccer firmly in the global spotlight, FIFA has scored an important — if incomplete — victory off the pitch. The EU's top court on Thursday declined to strike down FIFA's rules governing soccer agents. Instead, it handed the final decision back to a German court, which will now have to decide whether the regulations comply with EU law. The case was brought by two soccer agents who challenged several of FIFA's rules, arguing they unfairly restrict competition and limit how agents can do business. The judges didn't decide whether FIFA's rulebook is legal or illegal. Instead, they said the German court must examine whether each restriction genuinely serves a legitimate purpose — such as protecting players or preventing conflicts of interest — and whether it goes no further than necessary. One rule, however, clearly caught the court's attention. FIFA generally prevents agents from approaching players or coaches who are already represented by another agent until the final two months of an exclusive contract. According to the judges, that rule appears to protect established agents from competition and could amount to an abuse if FIFA holds a dominant position in the market. That doesn't mean the rule automatically falls. Nor does it spell the end of FIFA's broader regulatory framework. Other controversial measures — including limits on agents' fees, licensing requirements and restrictions on representing multiple parties in the same transfer — could still survive. But they will now have to clear a stricter legal test before the German court. For FIFA, that's enough to keep its rulebook alive. For soccer agents challenging the regulations, the judgment offers a clear roadmap for attacking the most restrictive parts.
articles.headline: FIFA's soccer-agent rules survive EU court ruling … for now

articles.body: NEW YORK — Trump’s first World Cup appearance could double as a diplomatic obstacle course. From Canada’s Mark Carney to Mexico’s Claudia Sheinbaum, the leaders in attendance could make for some awkward moments after months of clashes with the president. Sophia Cai reports from the final.
articles.headline: The awkward encounters ahead of Trump

articles.body: Europeans are much less likely than Americans to support the upcoming half-time show, according to recent polling by POLITICO. Half-time shows are a tradition in the Super Bowl, America's flagship sporting encounter, but FIFA is bringing the fête to the World Cup for the first time Sunday. The imminent performance will be headlined by music superstars Justin Bieber, Madonna, Shakira and K-pop band BTS. On Tuesday, the BBC reported that the show could extend the halftime break for up to 25 minutes, longer than the standard 15-minute half-time. Just over half of U.K. respondents said they would oppose a half-time show, preferring that the U.S. and FIFA stick to soccer traditions. This was higher than other European countries polled, including Germany (48 percent), France (41 percent) and Spain (36 percent), but much higher than the mere 28 percent of Americans who were opposed. Unsurprisingly, older respondents were consistently less supportive of the half-time show than young people. Just 18 percent of 65+ Germans and Brits supported a show, compared to 45 percent and 42 percent of 18-24 year olds, respectively. FIFA chief Gianni Infantino said in May that the show will “touch the hearts of the people.”
articles.headline: Europeans say ugh to half-time show

articles.body: Mikie Sherrill has embraced the World Cup like few other Democratic governors, save perhaps Gov. Josh Shapiro in Pennsylvania. She has been to seven matches, built a tight relationship with the former standout U.S. goalkeeper Tony Meola and will take in today’s final at MetLife Stadium in New Jersey. (She missed one match because she was working on her first budget). “There’s a lot of controversy over whether or not this makes sense. We'll be doing all the math as these returns come in,” Sherrill said. “When I came into office, I really was focused on how to make this a net benefit for the state, and how to bring everyone into the state. If you're going to host this game where one in three people in the world will be watching this final, you want the people of New Jersey to feel like they're part of it, and not just that there are some elite people going to watch the game, but they're actually able to take part in the excitement, and these fan zones have been really wonderful for that as well.” Six months into her term, Sherrill spoke exclusively with POLITICO to talk about her battles with FIFA, President Donald Trump and, also, how she ended up in a puddle of beer doing the viral Viking Row celebration with an army of Norway fans outside of MetLife. The interview has been lightly edited for length and clarity.  How did you end up in that puddle of beer? I think we've seen all over the internet people who are loving our delis and our Wawas and enjoying the shore, and I think it's just been such a fantastic feeling. And we had Norway come, and as you've seen on social media, they were rowing. I rowed at Navy my freshman and sophomore year at Navy, so I had a little bit of a kinship there to all the rowers coming from Norway. So when they were coming back to the stadium, we reached out and said, "Hey, are you guys rowing? We want to come touch base and welcome you to the state. And they said, ‘Yeah, come row with us.’ So I did, and it was really a lot of fun.” What have your battles been like with FIFA, particularly on cost-of-living issues? I would say everything we've gotten out of the World Cup, we built. I would really like to leverage it — if the Women's World Cup is going to be in the U.S. — I would really like to leverage a little bit of what a special place it is to host a World Cup game here. And really try to leverage that for better benefits for the state because FIFA put nothing into this. I mean they're making $11 billion and now they're trying to sell grass, and they're trying not to pay their bills. It's really a problem. So you’d be open to being a host site again in 2031? If it made sense economically, yes. President Donald Trump — whom you have battled — will be at the final on Sunday. How are you going to handle that? What I think has been interesting is I think a lot of people went into Trump's second administration thinking they had learned how to handle him in his first administration, and the word on the street seemed to be you just do an insane amount of flattery, and then get what you want, and just you know kind of work that way. You know, keep your head down, don't say too much, just flatter the hell out of him, and then I'll give you what you want. And I don't think that has been the case in the second administration. And so we in New Jersey had a year of watching him operate, and every promise he made, he walked back. And so the art of the deal to Trump seems to be. Figure out what you want, leverage the heck out of you, and never give it to you. But try to have you sell your soul in the process. I just largely have focused on delivering for the people of New Jersey. And when he tries to punish people here in New Jersey, like when he tried to stop the Gateway Tunnel funding, when he gave out pink slips to the 1,000 workers that were already on the job, good union jobs. I didn't try to cut some deal with him. I didn't say I was going to name Penn Station after him or something because it wasn't his money to leverage. I just took him to court almost immediately, and we won. And then he appealed it, and we won again. And now he's, you know, said he's going to build some mass detention facility in Roxbury in a warehouse that only has two bathrooms, and he thinks he's going to put 2000 people in it. And there's no sewersystem, so we took him to court, and we won. So it's not that we're throwing gratuitous punches here. I'm just constantly fighting for the people of New Jersey, and when he attacks them, I'm going to stand up. What are your predictions for the final on Sunday? Is Argentina going to prevail? How many goals is Messi going to score? What are some things that you'd make Kalshi bets on ? I don't do Kalshi bets. But there are a lot of people from South America that live in New Jersey. A lot of our citizens have family there, and they're going to kill me because nobody down there likes Argentina, right? But I will tell you, I… I feel like Argentina with Messi playing for the MLS. It's just it's almost like the closest thing we were getting this year to a hometown team.
articles.headline: 'They're trying not to pay their bills': Sherrill unhappy with FIFA as World Cup wraps up

articles.body: POLITICO asked some of the most dedicated tourney followers in American politics to share their predictions for the final. New York Gov. Kathy Hochul: “Coming from Buffalo, we root for the underdogs, those who have not had the championship. Argentina’s had their opportunity. When was the last time Spain won? That was a long time ago.” Rob Bonta, California attorney general, former Yale soccer player and father to professional soccer player Reina Bonta: “The South American champs vs. the European Champs. Messi vs Yamal. The current 39-year-old GOAT vs the 19-year-old wunderkind. Argentina vs Spain. Argentina has come from behind in the final minutes multiple times and grittily found a way to defend their World Cup title, led by the little magician #10. Spain dismantled France with their tiki-taka/rondo possession, tactical dominance and beautiful football on full display. Two teams at the top of their game, playing for the most coveted trophy in football. It should be a final for the ages! I’m so conflicted! Love them both.” San Francisco Mayor Daniel Lurie: “1-1 after extra time, Argentina wins on penalties. After what we have seen in three knockout games from Messi and his team, they will win back-to-back, and he will be considered the greatest player of all time, surpassing Maradona.” Mia Bonta, assemblymember and mother to professional soccer player Reina Bonta: “I’m rooting for Argentina, the team including Messi have just played a phenomenal, gritty tournament. But most Bontas are thinking Spain is going to walk away with the trophy.” Tammy Murphy, former first lady of New Jersey and current chair of the World Cup host committee: “As we approach the final, I’m wearing two hats. As chair of the New York New Jersey FIFA World Cup Host Committee, my focus is on delivering an exceptional experience for everyone — from seamless transportation and strong security to a safe, welcoming atmosphere and a lasting economic impact for our region. As a fan of the game, it’s hard not to get emotional knowing we’re about to witness Match 104 after an incredible 39-day tournament. While I have to admit I was disappointed to see England fall in the semifinal, given my family’s English roots on my mother’s side, it’s hard not to be excited about a historic first: the reigning European champions facing the reigning South American champions in a World Cup final. It has all the makings of an unforgettable match. Game on!” San Jose Mayor Matt Mahan: “Given San Jose’s Spanish roots as the first official pueblo in ‘Alta California,’ I’m going to call it 2-1 for Spain with a late-match Argentinian surge that falls just short.” Tom Steyer, Democratic megadonor, former California gubernatorial candidate and former Yale soccer player: Spain, 2-1. Randy Villegas, a Bernie Sanders-backed progressive running against GOP Rep. David Valadao: Spain, 2-1. State Sen. Ben Allen, chair of the Senate Special Committee on International Sporting Events: Could not comment because he was too devastated by England’s loss to Argentina. New Jersey Gov. Mikie Sherrill: " In my heart, I just feel connected … to Argentina. In my head, I guess just watching Spain’s midfield, it’s hard to imagine that that doesn’t prevail." Sen. Todd Young (R-Ind.): 2-1 Argentina, with a goal and an assist for Lionel Messi.
articles.headline: Spain or Argentina? Politicians are divided on the World Cup final

articles.body: NEW YORK — There’s no bots about it. The New York City-themed World Cup jerseys announced by Mayor Zohran Mamdani in June and released online from July 8 to July 16 sold out every morning within minutes, though the Mamdani administration tells POLITICO there was no foul play involved. Frustrated shoppers suspected scalpers using automated bots to buy the jerseys within seconds during each morning’s release at 10 a.m., but New York’s Department of Citywide Administrative Services said it only detected two out of 3,500 orders that came from bots. “The NYC soccer jerseys have been tremendously popular—thousands have attempted to purchase through CityStore online every day. One thousand very real, very happy New Yorkers have already picked up their jerseys in person—we have not seen widespread bot activity,” a DCAS spokesperson said in a statement. The two detected bot orders were canceled, the spokesperson added. The jerseys’ high demand and low supply is a more likely explanation for why they sold out so quickly. The website limited releases to 500 jerseys every morning, and averaged 25,000 visitors to the store during the week it was for sale. DCAS said it used Shopify’s bot prevention services such as CAPTCHA tests and required in-person pick-ups to prevent automated purchases.
articles.headline: Mamdani’s NYC World Cup jerseys beat the bots

articles.body: 66% Sixty-six percent of Spaniards say they would feel pride if their country won the World Cup, just under Canada’s 67 percent for the highest level recorded in countries covered by the latest cross-border POLITICO Poll. (The United States was lowest, at 50 percent.) As was the case in other European countries surveyed, supporters of Spain's right-of-center parties are the most likely to say pride would accompany soccer victory, including 83 percent of likely voters for Spain's opposition People’s Party. Follow other findings from the latest POLITICO poll  here .
articles.headline: Why Spain's right is ready to cheer

articles.body: Our Adam Wren spent the last six weeks exploring the summer's most unlikely fling: between Donald Trump's MAGA movement and the communitarian, cosmopolitan sport whose flagship event he embraced. You can read Adam's POLITICO Magazine piece, "America First, Soccer Second" here .
articles.headline: Did MAGA ever really fall in love with the world’s game?

articles.body: Ireland is hoping the global sporting spotlight surrounding the World Cup will also shine on its growing sports technology industry. Enterprise Ireland, the Irish government’s trade and innovation agency, has been working to help Irish companies expand in the U.S., from sports analytics firms to the Irish company behind the natural grass used at nine World Cup stadiums. POLITICO spoke with Aidan McKenna, Enterprise Ireland’s head of U.S. operations, about Ireland’s sports tech ecosystem and how Trump administration’s trade policies have shaped the agency’s approach. This interview has been edited for length and clarity. Ireland has developed a reputation in sports technology. Why has the sector become such a strength? I think there’s probably two big aspects to this. It’s probably just our passion for sports, all types of sports: soccer, rugby, our own national Gaelic football. But we also, given our cultural connections to the U.S., we have a really good understanding of U.S. games. We have a growing number of technology companies that are looking to bring data and health science into the professional area of sports. An Irish company is also behind the playing surface at several World Cup venues. Tell us about that. McKenna: SIS Pitches originated in Ireland. They designed a way to grow natural grass and sod in a certain environment that can be lifted and transplanted into these stadiums.They’ve become world leaders in kind of natural grass solutions for stadiums all over the world. It is really nice to say that an Irish company is behind growing the best possible surface for our world-class players at the World Cup, and nine stadiums have used their solution. Beyond the World Cup, what role do you see Ireland playing around the 2028 Los Angeles Olympics? We’re a proud sporting nation. We punch above our weight. We will have our top athletes come to LA, and I know we’re going to be based in Long Beach, and we’re going to ensure that the Irish athletes are supported. How has the Trump administration’s approach to Europe and trade changed how Enterprise Ireland thinks about investing or expanding in the U.S.? Enterprise Ireland, or indeed Ireland and the EU, would always advocate against any trade barriers. Where there’s tariffs, or non-tariff barriers, it makes the opportunity for trade more difficult. But the U.S. is a fantastic market. It’s a huge market. It’s the largest market in the world. What we’ve done in Enterprise Ireland is remind the U.S. administration that Irish companies are making a contribution to the U.S. The top 10 Irish companies employ 125,000 Americans in highly highly skilled jobs here. Has that environment changed what it means to be an Irish exporter? We would always advocate: if you want to do business, you have to be closer to customers, you have to give your customers confidence that you’re close to them. We have always advocated that to be successful in the overseas market, you got to be committed, you got to be in the market. I’m actually actively fast-tracking the amount of Irish companies that are establishing a presence in the U.S. So in New York, we’ve done what we call a landing pad accelerator so companies can get the right legal advice, get the right tax advice, develop your go-to-market strategy, hire the right people. And we encourage the founders of the Irish businesses to come in and spend a couple of years here, carry their culture, bring that into the U.S. teams. Finally, what role can sports play in strengthening U.S.-Irish ties? Sport is a brilliant bridge. It’s a brilliant arbitrator of bringing people together... We’re going to host the Ryder Cup in Adare Manor in September 2027. We’re going to open up Ireland to businesses, to people, to golf enthusiasts. The World Cup itself — it’s a fantastic celebration of what’s good in the world.
articles.headline: Ireland's World Cup play

articles.body: Looming over the World Cup final is another rivalry: the bitter feud between Argentine President Javier Milei and Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez. For the past two years, Argentina's libertarian-populist leader has been embroiled in a war of words with his progressive counterpart in Madrid. Milei has resented Sánchez's efforts to cast him as a far-right firebrand, and responded by repeatedly insulting the Spanish premier and his wife, Begoña Gómez, whom he has repeatedly called "corrupt." In Buenos Aires, one leading political observer doesn't expect the president to mix the World Cup final with his personal animus toward Sánchez. "I can't imagine him trying to turn this match into a political issue," Martín Rodríguez Yebra, deputy managing editor of Argentina’s leading conservative newspaper La Nación, told POLITICO . "It's not in his interest." According to Rodríguez Yebra, who has spent years following the Sánchez-Milei feud, Argentina's leader has identified Sánchez — one of the world's most recognized left-leaning political figures — as a useful antagonist in his "cultural battle" against " woke forces around the world ." "He uses Sánchez almost as a punching bag to demonstrate that he's confronting the international left," he said, adding that the tactic plays well among Milei's most devoted followers. But Rodríguez Yebra noted that for average Argentinians "who likely wouldn't recognize Sánchez if he sat next to them in a café in Buenos Aires," the contretemps is "a squabble that is seen as a random distraction" on most days, and which would be considered totally inappropriate to bring up in the almost sacred context of the World Cup final. "There are no words to accurately describe the idolatry people feel for our current national squad, which is perhaps the most admired in modern Argentine history," he said. "And they — and in particular [Lionel] Messi, in his role as the squad's captain — have made clear that they will not tolerate attempts at politicization." In previous editions of the World Cup, Rodríguez Yebra said, Argentina's national team had become mixed up with the country's politics. The squad is widely considered to have been instrumentalized as a propaganda tool when the country hosted the 1978 tournament, when the government was in the hands of a military junta that carried out the forced disappearances of tens of thousands of political dissidents. A few years later, legendary squad captain Diego Maradona didn't hide his close relationship with center-left populist President Carlos Menem, with whom he appeared on the balcony of the Casa Rosada presidential palace in Buenos Aires following the country's 1986 World Cup win. Menem would eventually make the striker his government's official "sports adviser" and even gave him a diplomatic passport usually reserved for high-ranking officials. Under Messi's leadership, however, the national team has taken steps to distance itself from Argentina's polarized political scene, and after winning the last edition of the World Cup, the squad declined an invitation from then-President Alberto Fernández to emulate Maradona and celebrate the victory at the presidential palace. Rodríguez Yebra said the current squad captain appeared to have been shaped by his lengthy professional career with FC Barcelona, which played out in parallel with the rise of the Catalan separatist movement and the unauthorized attempt to declare the Spanish region's independence in 2017. "Despite the enormous pressure to do so, he never waded into the issue, and as captain for Argentina he's taken the same approach and stayed on message," he said. "Don't mix us up in your politics: We're athletes and we represent the entire country, not politicians." The La Nación journalist pointed out that following the semifinal match against England, Messi stayed away when some of the victorious Argentine players unfurled a banner reading “ Las Malvinas son Argentinas ” (“The Falkland Islands are Argentinian”). "He doesn't want to be involved," he said. Rodríguez Yebra said Milei is a canny politician who attempts to align himself with overwhelming public sentiment and knows how to be pragmatic. That's why his war of words with Sánchez has ultimately not had a significant impact on Spanish-Argentine relations. "Despite the inflammatory rhetoric between our leaders, the high-level officials below them have continued working together on trade, investment and international issues, including the recently concluded EU-Mercosur trade agreement ," he said. "Ministers continue meeting, and diplomatic channels remain open even as the presidents trade barbs," he added. "Milei insults Sánchez when it suits him, and right now, given the team's insistence to keep politics out of the match, it is not in his interest to do so." On Sunday, Argentina's president will be playing the part of cheerleader-in-chief from Buenos Aires. While Sánchez will watch the match in person in New York , Milei will remain at home in an act of political superstition. "In 1990 Menem traveled to Italy to watch our team play against Cameroon and he ended up presiding over our most shocking World Cup defeat," Rodríguez Yebra said. "The episode was politically devastating for him, because it caused him to develop a reputation for being jinxed, and since then our presidents have steered clear of the matches." Rodríguez Yebra said Milei has every interest in doing whatever he can to ward off bad luck. Rising unemployment and corruption cases involving close aides have eroded the president's approval ratings, with around 60 percent of voters saying they disapprove of his performance. While the lack of a serious political rival means that the populist remains the clear front-runner ahead of next year's presidential election, an Argentine win on Sunday would still be a godsend. The same goes for Sánchez, should La Roja emerge victorious. Unlike Argentina, Spain’s unemployment is at a record low and the country’s economy is among the fastest-growing in Europe. But like Milei, the prime minister is grappling with damaging corruption scandals . Last month, the country’s former transport minister was handed a 24-year prison sentence for bribery, embezzlement and influence peddling, and earlier this week, Sánchez’s brother was found guilty of administrative misconduct after a regional court concluded he had been unfairly awarded a public sector position. "This World Cup has been a welcome distraction that has already helped him enormously, and could benefit him even further if it results in our team's victory," Rodríguez Yebra said about Milei. "Even without engaging in politics, a win for our team is a win for him."
articles.headline: Following Messi's lead, Milei bids to separate stadium and state
